name: Goll mac Morna
description: Goll mac Morna, also known as Conán mac Morna, is a complex figure in Irish mythology, often portrayed as a powerful warrior and leader of the Fianna, a band of legendary Irish heroes. His origins are steeped in ambiguity, with some accounts depicting him as a noble figure and others as a treacherous usurper. He is known for his one eye, lost in a battle, which earned him the epithet 'Goll' meaning 'one-eyed'. Goll's most significant act is his involvement in the death of Cumhall, the father of Fionn mac Cumhaill, an event that sets the stage for a long and often adversarial relationship between Goll and Fionn. Despite his role in Cumhall's death, Goll is not always depicted as purely villainous. He is often portrayed as a formidable warrior, fiercely loyal to his own men, and capable of acts of great bravery. He sometimes serves as a reluctant ally to Fionn, demonstrating a sense of honor and duty, even amidst their complicated history. His character highlights the complexities of heroism in Irish myth, where loyalty, vengeance, and fate intertwine to create multifaceted and compelling figures. Goll's strength and martial prowess are undeniable, and his story serves as a reminder that even those who commit questionable acts can possess admirable qualities. His legacy is one of strength, ambition, and the enduring consequences of past actions.
subtitle: One-eyed warrior of the Fianna.

keyMyths.title: The Death of Cumhall
keyMyths.description: Goll mac Morna kills Cumhall, the father of Fionn mac Cumhaill, leading to a lifelong feud between Goll and Fionn. This act establishes Goll's complex character and sets the stage for future conflicts.

keyMyths.title: Goll's One Eye
keyMyths.description: Goll earns his name, meaning 'one-eyed,' after losing an eye in battle. This injury becomes a defining characteristic and a symbol of his battle-hardened nature.

keyMyths.title: Goll and Fionn's Relationship
keyMyths.description: Despite their initial animosity, Goll and Fionn sometimes find themselves as allies, highlighting the complex dynamics of loyalty and duty within the Fianna. Their interactions showcase the nuances of heroism.

sources.author: Standish Hayes O'Grady
sources.description: A collection of Irish tales and historical narratives.
sources.text: A collection of Irish tales and historical narratives.
